did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), a deep learning method, to classify benign and malignant melanomas from skin lesion images. Specifically, it trained a sequential CNN on a repository of 1000 images from the ISIC dataset to achieve 94.89% accuracy, and a second CNN on 600 images with added metadata to achieve 98.39% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors developed and trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on a custom repository of 1000 and 600 images from the ISIC dataset to classify benign and malignant melanomas. It reports specific new results (94.89% and 98.39% accuracy) and proposes a novel method of adding metadata to the pixel matrix, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: CNN and Metadata for Classification of Benign and Malignant Melanomas
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of benign and malignant melanomas to support early detection of skin cancer. The authors utilized Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on image data sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) repository. In the first instance, a sequential CNN was trained on a repository of 1000 images without metadata, achieving 94.89% accuracy. In the second instance, the authors proposed a novel method of embedding patient metadata (sex, age, and lesion size) directly into the image's pixel matrix as a color band, training on a dataset of 600 images. This approach resulted in an improved accuracy of 98.39%, demonstrating that adding metadata to the pixel matrix enhances classification performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification targets in the experiment. Included in the repository of 1000 images (600 training, 200 validation) and the 600-image metadata repository (200 training, 100 validation).

One of the two binary classification targets in the experiment. Included in the repository of 1000 images (600 training, 200 validation) and the 600-image metadata repository (200 training, 100 validation).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: benign melanoma vs. malignant melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is the 'classification of benign and malignant melanomas'. The model predicts one of two discrete classes (benign or malignant) using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Performance is measured by accuracy (94.89% and 98.39%) and validation accuracy, which are standard metrics for classification tasks, not regression.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A public repository of melanoma images and metadata available at isic-archive.com, used as the source for the study's image packages.

use.training: Used to create two image packages: one without metadata (1000 images total: 600 training, 400 validation) and one with metadata (600 images total: 200 training, 200 validation). Metadata (sex, age_approx, clin_size_long_diam_mm) was embedded into the pixel matrix.
use.validation: Used for validation in both instances (400 images without metadata, 200 images with metadata).
use.testing: Used for testing with images not included in training or validation (results: 61% accuracy for Instance 1, 85% for Instance 2). Specific counts for this test set were not reported.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy and validation accuracy metrics without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC_Images_without_metadata.notes: Training performed with 8 Epochs, 350 height, 350 weight, 128 batch_size, 2 classes, 1000 step_train, 300 step_validation, 32 filter_Conv1, 64 filter_Conv2, (3, 3) size_Filter, (2, 2) size_Filter2, and (2,2) size_Pool. Final epoch results reported.

ISIC_Images_with_metadata.notes: Metadata (sex, age_approx, clin_size_long_diam_mm) embedded in pixel matrix. Training parameters same as Instance 1. Final epoch results reported.

ISIC_Test_Set.notes: Tested on images not included in training or validation. Specific count of test images not reported.

Comparison_with_Literature.notes: Results from related works included for comparison in Table 3. The paper notes that results of a test with new images were not found in the related works.

architecture_details.metadata_integration: Metadata (sex, age_approx, clin_size_long_diam_mm) was duplicated and inserted into the pixel matrix as a color band at the bottom of the image (rows 300 to 350) across all three RGB channels.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Yang (DCNN multitasking model based on GoogleNet)
Shoieb (pre-trained CNN with SVM multiclass classifier)
Dorj (deep convolutional neuronal network with ECOC-SVM classifier)
Oliveira (KNN, Bayes net, C4.5, MLP, SVM)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 350 x 350 pixels for input to the CNN
Embedding patient metadata (sex, age_approx, clin_size_long_diam_mm) directly into the image's pixel matrix as a color band at the bottom (rows 300 to 350) across all three RGB channels
conclusions: Artificial neural networks can help in the creation of applications for the classification of benign and malignant melanomas.
Including metadata in a duplicate way within the same pixel matrix enriches the extraction of characteristics and improves classification results.
The proposal to add metadata directly to the image matrix improves the classification of melanomas.
The authors plan to continue testing with more metadata to identify the most significant metadata features.
The technique of adding metadata to the pixel matrix could potentially be used for the classification of other types of images.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset for the metadata-enhanced model (Instance 2), using only 600 images total (200 training, 200 validation), which limits statistical power and generalizability.

There is a significant discrepancy between training accuracy (98.90%) and validation accuracy (79.00%) for the metadata model, indicating potential overfitting despite the authors' claim that validation accuracy remained 'stable'.

The test set performance was notably lower than validation performance (85% on test vs 79% on validation for Instance 2), and the specific size or composition of this external test set was not reported.

The method of embedding metadata as a color band at the bottom of the image (rows 300-350) introduces spatial bias, potentially allowing the CNN to learn from the metadata location rather than lesion features alone.

The paper does not report standard diagnostic metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, or AUC, relying solely on accuracy, which is insufficient for evaluating clinical utility in imbalanced or high-stakes diagnostic tasks.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance improvement over baseline or other methods.

The comparison with related works is limited because the authors note that results for 'new images' (the test set) were not available in the cited literature, preventing a fair head-to-head comparison on identical data.

The dataset source (ISIC) is public, but the specific selection criteria for the 1000 and 600 images are not detailed, raising concerns about potential selection bias or data leakage if the test set overlaps with public ISIC subsets used in other studies.
